Agents will inevitably become direct participants in the economy.

Not just tools or assistants, but direct actors that do work on your behalf.

Check what these guys are doing:

You can package your professional skills into autonomous agents that find work and collaborate with

Right now, I have a few dozen tools to automate my life.

Every single thing that I do more than once every week is now a tool: either a Claude Code skill, a scheduled workflow, or an application.

• I spend the time doing this once.
• I schedule it.
• I forget about it (or at

The 4 things I really appreciate about CREAO:

1. It can integrate with any API by pointing to its documentation. This saves a ton of time.

2. Once you build a workflow, it runs the same way every single time. Deterministic outputs are important for many of the things I do.

3.

The same AI that wrote your slop code is now reviewing it.

Please, don't call this "code reviews". This is like you grading your own homework.

Every AI tool has blind spots. Every tool makes assumptions, takes shortcuts, and misses edge cases.

What do you think will happen

This is a huge conflict of interest, and you have to be blind not to see it.

Code generation and code review are fundamentally different jobs.

Code generation is about producing something that works. Code reviews are about finding the ways that code might break.

Vibe-code is

Custom models aren't optional anymore. They are inevitable.

For many use cases, using out-of-the-box models is not optimal.

But of course, building these custom models is not easy or cheap. Most teams don't even have enough people or time to do this.

Check out what

Anything that could be automated should be automated.

I've been using 
@CreaoAI
 for a while. I've built and scheduled about a dozen tasks using their agents.

The reason they are good: they produce deterministic tools, so the same input will always produce the same output.

Things I'm happy I invested time in when I was younger:

- Working out
- Building relationships
- Supporting people I believe in
- Doing well at jobs that mattered
- Taking care of my physical health

All of these have compounded massively.

The happiest people I know all made a choice that looked unreasonable at some point. 

Left a great job. Turned down a raise. Moved somewhere without a plan.

Sometimes the choices that look bad lead to lives that feel great.

If you've never done creative work, assume the role of the machine gunner.

Think, write, and publish, over and over again. Don't stop for a year.

Once you know what you like writing, what resonates, and how to say it, become the sniper.

You can learn anything in 2 weeks.

You can't master it, obviously, but if you obsess over it, you can become better at it than most people ever will. You'd be surprised how fast your life can change when you understand this.
